Hi, guys. I'm Christine here with Bond, Mark, Kevin, David, and our very special guest today, incredibly successful and beloved copywriter, Michael Fortin. And as always, if you have questions for Michael or for anyone on the panel, by all means, type them in the chat and we will get to them as soon as we can. And we are going to find out as many of the secrets as we can as to how this man has made so much money for so many people. So I'm going to hand things over to Mark McCray to start the conversation. Thanks, Christine. And as we mentioned before, when Kevin and Bond and I were putting together the people that are a dream team of people that we would like to invite into halberdology to share the wealth of experience, one of the very first names that we came up with was Michael Fortin. Not only because of his relationship with Gary Halber, but because of just how great a copywriter Michael is. And my first introduction to Michael was when I read the Traffic Secrets sales page that launched John Halber, John Reese's million dollars and 24 hours. So welcome, Michael. Well, thank you. Michael, for the people who are listening, how did you get involved and how did you get started in copywriting? What did you do before and what led you to decide that this is what you wanted to do for a living? Well, it's actually kind of a story that leads into another and a very long story, very short. What I did was I jumped into sales because I had this immense fear of rejection when I was a kid and partly because I grew up with an alcoholic father and partly because I just found out I have Asperger's, where social cues sometimes are something that have been a prevalent symptom amongst Aspies. So, you know, I didn't I didn't have a chance to or I didn't have a a good sales technique. So I learned everything about sales and they still hated rejection. I still hated knocking on doors and getting the door slam in my face. So I decided one day, you know, and I declared bankruptcy. Actually, I was about 21 years old and I was, you know, using credit cards in order to buy groceries for my family. And then one day I decided I said, OK, if I just write a letter, I write a letter and I send it to people who then call me to book an appointment so I can make a sales presentation. I won't have to be rejected anymore. And then I went from a bankrupt insurance salesperson to becoming the number one insurance salesperson for eight months in a row in a Fortune-O-Pattern Corporation. So I realized, huh, there's something to this copywriting thing. So I decided to I kept on doing sales and I learned everything about sales and everything just translated into, you know, to copy because a lot of people say, well, what is copywriting? Well, if you learn everything about sales and you learn all the sales techniques, writing copies is just basically a written form of doing a good sales presentation. And no, there's no person better that has regurgitated that so often than Gary Halper. It's just salesmanship and print. True. Fantastic. And how did the, how long had you been writing copy before the John Reeves sales letter? I, when I first started in copywriting, well, my first official job as a copywriter was with a cosmetic surgeon and actually a hair transplant surgeon back in 1992. Although I've been doing it with other clients and other employers, I'm not clients, but employers, it wasn't really my first official job. But my first job was in 1992. And I wrote John Reeves's traffic secrets letter in 2004. Yeah, because it was launched on August 17th, 2004. That was the famous million dollar day where John sort of broke the barrier, you know, and I want to make something absolutely clear. A lot of people say, well, Michael Fortune, you're a million dollar copywriter. You wrote the sales letter that made a million dollars in one day. I have to admit that that has been broken many, many times since by copywriters out there who I believe are much better than I am. But I guess you can say that I'm sort of the Roger Bannister of copy. I'm the guy who wrote the first sales letter that broke the four minute mile and so to speak. I'm the guy who wrote the first sales letter that made a million dollars in one day. Because after that, I did it again. I think it was, you know, about a month later for another client. I know guys like Frank Kern and tons of other fantastic copywriters have beaten that so many times. So I'm proud to have been the first one, but it's I'm not the only one. And I just make that clear. I wish I still had that letter actually, because it was so good. I remember copying that. And, you know, but so long ago, you moved from computer to computer. But I read that thing about 10 times. It was a fantastic letter. And Gary, Gary Halberst, how did you meet Gary? Oh, my. I think what happened was I did, I was doing a lot of seminars and I was doing seminars on copywriting and I was doing it with some other copywriters. And I think I've met John Carleton. It was at an event and John introduced me to Gary and very much the rest is history. Oh. We're still all here. We can still hear you. Yeah. My audio connection disappeared for a second again. I think you're fine. Just not. It's fine. Just not my call. Okay. Good. I was just saying that I met John Carleton at an event and John Carleton introduced me to Gary and that one thing led to another. So that's how I met Gary. I remember, I remember doing seminars with Gary and we were done and people were asking him questions. He would just get on stage and tell these stories about and everything that he would say, everything's, every story that he would tell, you think there's not, you know, you think he's going on a tangent. You think that he's not really making the point, but then when he comes at the end of this story, it wraps it all up beautifully. I mean, that's, that's the brilliance of Gary Helberg was his story at the end made sense and it made a point and it made a point effectively because at the end of the story, you say, huh, right. I mean, of course Gary had a lot of very colorful stories to tell. But, but a lot of the stories also are not like that. And in fact, we were talking offline before with all the microphone mix up. Gary used to call me on the phone just because he wanted to respond to some people on the, on the forum and he didn't know how. So he called me on the phone and asking me how to type in what he wanted me to type in and I said, look, Gary, just tell me the story. I will type it in for you. He says, man, you were awesome. So, and then, and we would do that. So I literally had to log out, log back in as him and then he would tell me the story and you literally dictate the story to me. And with a few choice words that I'm not going to repeat on this podcast, but, but basically that's what, that's what made, I think Gary Helberg a true, he was a story seller, not a storyteller, because telling stories is one thing. And, and being able to capture people's attention for that story is another. But then a third is to be able to tell a story, captures people's attention and get them to take action. And I think Gary was just brilliant at doing that. I think it's a good time to bring in maybe Kevin or Bond here. Kevin, have you got a question for Michael? No, I just, I was just going to make the observation that, once again, you know, a successful copywriter has been a face-to-face salesman. And I think there's just something that comes into the idea that you can, I think there's, I think the interesting thing, which I think you'll notice is that when you're selling someone face-to-face, they can be saying nothing to you. And you know, when you're looking at their face, you know, I got them. I just turned them around. He's not on his back foot anymore. Now he's interested. Have you noticed that? Like when you were doing the face-to-face, when you're getting past rejection, there was a thing and you, and it, this is, this is the thing. Back, back in the day, I used to be a photographer and we would shoot, shoot models and the beginners were really hard to get used to. And I remember I was, we were working one day, I was assistant on another with a supermodel and stuff like that. And the thing was, is that the models kind of got this Pavlovian response because when we were photographing them, uh, the, when the model did something that was really, uh, uh, special, the camera would go off and the motor drive would go off and the models would kind of learn by every photographer they worked with, what certain poses would make that camera go off. And I just think that, you know, uh, there's something about the way you talk to somebody that you remember, what that turning point is for when that, that person goes and so, uh, writing, writing copy, uh, just, uh, without ever having a face-to-face thing, you don't get that experience of knowing when that magic moment happens. And I just think it's always interesting that, uh, all these successful ex face-to-face salesmen have got that feedback that somehow, I don't know what it does, but it somehow makes them know how to even write for a whole other different topic in a way that gets them to do it. That's just more of an observation than a question. You just did it now. You just used the camera analogy. You know, and, and, and Gary, that's what Gary was doing. That's, it's, it's a very powerful process in sales. Definitely. I, you know, the, the, what I want to add to it, which is, uh, you know, because here you talk about our father, you know, it always trips back a memory to me and, you know, when you talk about storytelling, you talk about his skillset. I think one thing very few people would be, I mean, everybody would be shocked to know is he wrote less than any copyright or I know, I mean, physically, right? Everything was going on in his head. And he explained everything in under two situations. If he had a great story, like the colorful stories, they were all true, by the way. And I mean 100% true. And you only heard, I mean, he only shared, you know, maybe 10% of what was really going on, because Kevin and I have this phrase, which is, uh, we always say, if you met Gary Halbert, you have a story and it's not just his story. You have a story of his behavior and the thing like, you can't believe you just saw him do this to somebody or so, so forth. But if he didn't have the story, he'd use a hypothetical. So, you know, in arguments and what, you know, when we were growing up, when we make a point and you make a point in person to somebody, we'd use a hypothetical say, okay, you know, for example, and he would always go to the ultimate. He would say, you know, okay, somebody's got a gun to your little girl's head. Okay. And this has to be done in this way. And he would get answers out of people that way. But the point that I want to add that Kevin was mentioning, which I think you can allude to is storytellers aren't great people who just practice typing and writing stories and getting, you know, having a few drinks on Saturday night. They live great stories. They share great stories in person. So if you really want to be a better copywriter, if you really want to be a better storyteller, start selling in person, start telling in person, start sharing, you know, doing stories in person and so forth. A lot of the copywriters that are the most fascinating and interesting, it's because they've lived really fascinating and interesting lives as well. There are exceptions to that. I'm not saying that you can't, you know, you can't just kind of model yourself after what we're doing. But I want to ask you, I mean, you use stories in your, in your selling and so forth, but don't you also find that when you're in a group of people, you're also one of these storytellers, like, you know, when you're all sitting at a coffee to shop or, you know, like, just like what we were doing before the camera started, we're sharing stories, you know, and it's that, that willingness and practice is where it counts. And that's why Gary did not write so much. He was practicing living his life and selling and telling. And so I want to ask you on your feedback on that, don't you find yourself to be one of these storytellers in the group when you're around? There's, there's a, an odd story that relates to Gary very often is whenever I would go to a seminar, the true seminar happens at the bar after, after the seminar is done. And very often Gary would do this and I would do this, but especially Gary, and it was, it was fascinating just to watch, not what he said, people's gaze and attention and how they just lean forward. I mean, Gary would be at a bar and there would be one, two, three people around him and he would tell stories. And then as the night progressed, there was like 25 people in the bar all around Gary listening to his stories. And that's the power of capturing people's attention and captivating them at the same time. And I think Gary embodied that very much. And you absolutely right. Because oftentimes I, I write copy, I will follow formulas, but I often have a story that I want to embed in there. And that's how I do it. I sort of look at what is the point, what is the ultimate end goal? What is the point I'm trying to make? Let me, let me backtrack a little bit. I'll tell you a story. I used to teach at Algonquin College, very local college here, a very well-known college around this area, in Ottawa, Canada. And I used to teach professional selling. And we came to the chapter about how the mind works. We, we think in pictures, we think in metaphors and analogies. And it's essentially what the point was, is that the mind thinks in relative terms. We don't think in absolutes. We don't think in words, actual words or picture or numbers. We actually think in pictures. And I was teaching class one time and this client, this kid was, he was a bit of a rebel in class and he comes up to me and he says, Professor Fortin, I want to ask you a question. What's the difference between this chair and this tennis ball? I said, well, one is to sit on and one is to play with. He says, no, that's not the difference. I mean, of course, I just give you the difference. No, you didn't give me the difference. You related the difference rather than saying the difference is their function. You explain their function. And you said the chairs to sit on and the ball is to play with. Okay. What's, what's the, what's the difference between a soccer ball and his tennis ball? I said, well, one is black and white and one is yellow. No, the difference is their color. You related the difference by explaining the H1 of their colors. And that clicked in my mind. I'm thanking myself. That's exactly how copy works because we, if we have, if we have the capability to think with incredible amount of empathy, because that's what copywriting requires. And also to think, like you were talking about, and I think it was Kevin, you're talking about not being able to see if cues. Well, when you tell a story, you can sort of visualize in your mind what the people, how people are reacting to your story, how they're, they're responding and how they're warming up to you and how they are actually going to take action based on what you're saying. And you know full well that when you're telling that story, you're going to get them involved in the story as well. It's something that I learned a lot when I was writing copy was the power of fiction, the power of storytelling. A great book that I often tell people to read for copywriting is Stephen King's On Writing. That book is amazing because it tells you about how to write stories, how to tell stories. And guess what? It applies to copy just as well. Fantastic. That book is, I have that dog-eared, highlighted, everything. That is one of my favorite go-to writing books. I'm so glad you mentioned that. And what about Niche's now, Michael? I mean, the John Reese was the sort of internet marketing niche. Have you stayed in that niche? Have you, have you had a broad range of clients or what have you been up to for the last few years? Oh, I've written copy for so many different industries, so many unrelated industries. But, but truth be told, whenever I wrote that copy for Traffic Secrets, my copy for internet marketers business pretty much exploded from that point on naturally. But I wrote copy for investors, for insurance companies. I've written copy for authors. I've written copy for cosmetic surgeons from, you know, hair transplants to, to tummy tucks. I've actually produced, I wrote infomercials for those doctors. I wrote copy for lawyers. Anyways, the bottom line is I've written for so many different industries. And you know what? It's a good question because it makes, it reminds me of something. A lot of people say, well, my industry is different. And I remember Gary actually talking about that when people say, well, I don't want to write like that because my, my industry is different. You know what? We might be in different industries, but all industries have one thing in common. Human beings. We are all, I mean, we were born with a disease called emotion. And so basically everybody, I mean, I can write copy for engineers who are very data, data focus, scientific, PhD level type of thinkers, but you know what? They still make decisions on emotion. They still make the emotional decisions. They still back up. They're, they'll probably do the opposite. You know, let people say, uh, make a decision based on emotion and justify it with logic. Many times you'll have the scientists, scientific people who will do a lot of logic in their, in their, in their decision making. But you know what? They still will use emotion to make your decisions or they'll back it up with emotion. So you have, that's the power of storytelling. That's the power of connecting no matter what industry you're in. You know, though, one of your recent posts and your blog was do what you love. Is there a niche that over the years you've found to be the one that, you know, if you get it, if you get a writing assignment, that niche, you kind of go, Oh, goodie. Here we go. Uh, for me as a writer, yeah. Um, not really. Well, yeah, I kind of, there's some niches that I absolutely love writing about because they're niches that actually connect with me. Here's an example. And I was walking, actually was doing seminars, walking on stage with a cane, sitting on a chair, uh, with a back brace. And I thought to myself, I've got to do something. Um, I got, I have to be there when I found out that, especially my wife was diagnosed with stage four cancer. I thought, okay, I have to be strong for her. And I've been doing, I've been doing weightlifting for three, but I've been powerlifting competitively for two. Um, I remember he tells a story about whenever he, his first business or his first sales company was, or first company he sold for was pots and pans. And they said, no, before you sell, you go buy one and try it out because you have to understand the product to fully appreciate how people need to appreciate it as well. And that's the point is that I write copy for an industry that I totally, I sometimes I, you know, John Carlton talks about how we are copywriters have to prostitute ourselves to a certain degree when we, when we write copy for an industry that we're not necessarily totally keen on, but that's the beauty of being busy as a copywriter is that I can pick and choose. Oh, Mark, you've been waiting. Go ahead. Actually you and Mark are test junkies, or at least you were, you know, red headlines versus black headlines. Any new epiphanies? Are you still into that? The, you want to hear something funny? What's, what's being tested right now more than anything else are funnels from social media networks, especially Facebook.
