So do I, do I, do I split test copy itself? I think, I think I've done so much now that I, and my clients, and I do a lot of testing, but I don't really talk a lot about my own testing because I come across a lot of tests done by my clients. When I write copy for clients, a lot of these guys are like top testers. So I'm privileged to have all this intimate knowledge of a lot of stuff that works, but, but now I don't do as much or I don't come across as many as I used to right now. What's more than anything else is the funnel system or the funnel process. And I, and I say funnel because, you know, that's a misnomer. People say, oh, it's a, you have to have a funnel or some people say you don't have to have a funnel. That's not what I really mean. I'm talking about just a lead generation process, especially from social media network. It's amazing. Facebook is a gold mine right now. And if you do it right, you can have some high quality leads produce some high quality sales, uh, if you, if you test and you do some, some tracking that way. So that's what I'm doing a lot of work. That's what I'm, I have clients who are doing a lot of that. And so it's, it's fascinating to me. Uh, but just to, to finish it off, one thing that I have found more than anything else is the multimedia. Uh, I talk about this in my, my report. I wrote a report in 2006 called the death of the sales letter. And the point was not that sales letters are dead. It's just that they're evolving. I think that people have to understand that when, when, uh, Gutenberg press was invented and then we were able to write letters or type letters, people would then write sales letters on type. And I say that, of course, I was done in schools before, but my point is, we know, we would type word, you know, with word processor type writers, we would write letters. I mean, God knows that the Gary Helbert letter is, is, is, is one of them. But here's the point is that we would, we would read that. And we were, we had to read it in such a way where we are only involving the visual aspect and then we have to sort of translate our minds about this person meant, whatever. Then radio and TV came along. So audio and then visuals came along, which is going to give us the ability to add another dimension, another sense, engaging in other sense in the copyright process. But when the internet came along, which basically has that ability, has the ability of being visual and auditory. I think the third element is what people are missing is the kinesthetic, which is the fact that now we have the ability to scroll, we have the ability to click, we have the ability to interact. And that's what my point was in that report was that you, you can engage more senses now with the internet. You don't, you know, a lot of people say, Oh, the sales letters on the internet are, they look like a direct mail piece. That was true when the internet first began, but now it's not true anymore. I think that people are used to that. I think, you know, we were talking offline about how Gary used to be a little technophobic and the internet was not a big thing for him. Well, I know that he changed his mind, but he still wanted to people to say, Hey, you should order offline, put a phone number on your, on your order form. And of course that's, that's understandable because we're so used to this old medium, but that's why copywriting has to, not copywriting itself, but the medium in which copy is, is being projected or, or, or communicated is evolving and we need as copywriters to adapt to that. So anyways, I, Michelle, don't you find that, you know, that's so true. And it also, I think, can lead to a lot more opportunity to disconnect. I won't go into the, what it was, but I, I watched a commercial on television, a national commercial for that they're spending millions of dollars on huge company, biggest disconnect I've ever seen him. And when it got to the end of the commercial, I said, what? I mean, it just, it had nothing to do with whatsoever. So, I mean, I'm just curious about your thoughts about that because we are using audio, we're using visual and now we're, we're having people interact with us and yet it's all starting from the same thing, what we sit down and write. That gives us a lot of opportunity to screw up to, to create a disconnect when they're seeing what they're hearing, what they're reading, what we're asking their action that we're asking them to take. Any thoughts? Well, if you're talking about a commercial on TV, I'm talking about a video sales letter or anything. I mean, anytime you're getting all these senses involved. Well, are you, here's the thing, are you doing it in order to be, to engage all the senses? Are you trying to do it just for that reason? Don't. Um, and, and I, and I hear you. I've, I've seen sales letters and video sales letters that are absolute and utter crap. Um, and then I've seen sales letters and videos that are just, I couldn't, I after the video was done, I'm like, was that it? That's all. Now we're just flew by. And it comes back to, it comes back to the initial point about copywriting. Gary talks about this, the grease slide, you know, the degree shoot. We have to, we can try to appeal to all the senses. We can appeal to more than just the, the written format, but as copywriters, we have to remember we're salespeople, we're storytellers. We also have to keep people captured and, and in this continuum. And the moment you disconnect, the moment you, because a lot of times when you say there's a disconnect, that's because the, the, the copywriter is just too focused on themselves rather than on the client or on the reader. Or on the audience is in this case. So I've seen sales letters where they were trying to be flashy and try to engage all the senses and all that, just for the sake of engaging all the senses. That doesn't mean you're going to sell crap. Um, so as long as you remain true to salesmanship, true to keeping people in this grease shoot. Um, and a lot of times people say, uh, should I use video with my sales letter and I asked them, well, what's the con, what's your conversion rate on your sales letter if they're using texts and it's really good. It's really high. I say, don't, don't use video. If it's working, it's, it was what your audience wants and it's what's working for them. Can you, you know, you want to test it? Sure, try to, you know, test it, but it doesn't necessarily mean that it's right for every niche in every industry. Uh, and I didn't necessarily mean that too, too. Whenever I wrote that report that it would be across the board. I think it really depends on who your audience is. And I'll give you an example. Uh, I write copy for, uh, a golf industry, but specifically in a, in an industry where most of the golfers all retired men, retired old men, you know, and these guys hate video with a passion. They would prefer to read a letter. So we either use, we use video for the training, but the video is actually done of the person, the trainer showing how to golf and we use that as sort of an example of what they get, but the actual copy, it's all written. And so we try to remain connected that way. I hope, I hope that answers your question, Dave. You know, we've talked a lot about story, um, throughout halbertology. I mean, that's, that's a huge focus. Um, I think a lot of times we talk so much of the importance of something and there's probably a lot of new copywriters or, or just business owners are trying to, to get a handle on how to make the copy they're having written for them better. Um, any tips, tricks, um, suggestions on how to really dig it and find the story. Um, so often, you know, and I think the closer you are to your product, the closer you are to your industry, the close, especially if you do this every day. I mean, if you're every day, you're sending things out to these people or you can send people out to bang on people's doors and get them to buy your products, you get too close. And I think a little how to, uh, what do you, what do you do when you're, you know, especially if you're looking for the story and there just isn't, you know, tons of research, there isn't tons of interesting things there. You know, you, the, uh, the story behind the, uh, the owner or the, the creator, whatever it's just dry as can be, um, the products, not that. I say, you know, we have a saying, uh, in, in the forex niche that a lot of these systems could be written on a cocktail napkin. I think they're all very similar. So to make something new and exciting and, and different, and especially if, if you run into a situation with a guy who is the front man, the creator of this is also this guy that says, Oh, I don't know. I, I, I trade. That's all I do. It's just nothing interesting. But how do you limit up a story when there just doesn't seem to be one? It, well, this, a lot of times people think that the story has to be from the owner or the author of the product or the copy. And you know what? A lot of, a lot of my best copy didn't come out from the owner or the, uh, the, uh, the author of the sale letter. I'll give you, let me give you a tip of something I do and that'll probably answer your question. I have to write copy for a client that is exactly like that. Basically the, I have a questionnaire. I have 25 questions. If you go to successdoctor.com, forward slash questions. That's my questionnaire that all my clients have to fill out. Um, and success doctor, by the way, is my agency. When I first started out, I was a copyrighted for a cosmetic surgeon. So I was called a success doctor because I helped doctors become successful. Um, but whenever, uh, I would have them fill out the questionnaire and they would barely give me any information. Of course, the key to coming up with ideas is to do more market research. The more market research you do, the more you dig, the more you try to get as much information as you can from your client. The more ideas will come out of the work work to you. But if not, let's say you're really stuck with a really dry client with not a big, very big story. Here's what I asked them to do. I asked them to give me, uh, names and phone numbers of some of their, their happiest clients. If not, we have to find those if we can't, if we don't have them. And once I get those, I call them up, I record the conversation. I tell them, I ask for their permission. And then I asked them questions about how they use a product. Now they think that this is just a survey on trying the product. Well, what I'm really doing is I'm trying to evoke emotion from them. I'm trying to get them to sell me on the product. And very often what I'll do is I'll ask a question, but I'll, I'll leave it hanging. I'll ask, I'll say, and, and I'll stop. Or that means that, but what else? And all those things will prompt, they'll, they'll prod them, they'll prompt them to expand. And, oh yeah, I, you know, I tried to product the other day and this happened. And, oh my God, my life changed. And, uh, so what you're doing is basically having them write your copy for you. Because what I do is I take that recording and I transcribe it. I got a good portion of my copy already written for me. So some of these stories can come from actual clients from the market itself. Not, don't, don't just limit yourself to just the owner of the company. John Carlton talks about the story of his one legged golfer, which is a very famous story, but he, he talks about the fact that that client was also very dry that he didn't know, know much about. Then he had to interview him for a lot more until he found out that this guy only had, you know, he found out about this trick from another golfer who had just one leg. So sometimes you just have to dig a little bit deeper. And that's hard. Sometimes I get that. Um, but if you have to go, go further, talk to suppliers, talk to actual clients, talk to other people who have bought either the product or by competing products and ask them why they bought or why they didn't buy. You'll, you'll get a lot of information that will spark some ideas that can have, can have you tell a story, but also at the same time, stories that help to connect with the audience and get them to buy. So don't discount the product of the, the, the, the, the, uh, the immensity and the importance of market research. A lot of people ask me, Michael, how, how much time do you spend on writing copy? I spend maybe 20, if not 10% of my time writing copy, 80% is spent on market research, just digging into the client browsing, you know, even browsing websites, reading some other copy or, or reading about what, uh, uh, people say about the product, uh, calling clients up, uh, digging as much as possible. So that's sort of my little trick. Detective work. A lot of detective work. It goes into copywriting for sure. Oh yeah. I think, I think a lot of copywriters who write copy, I think Eugene Schwartz talks about this whenever he said he used to write copy and he was given a book, uh, from, uh, that Chinese guy. I can't remember the story, but it was, he was writing copy for a Chinese martial artist and literally like the book was filled with, with notes and sticky notes and dog ear and, and, and scribbles. And he would literally spend so much time on that book that then, then he would write the copy and it would just flow from that point on. But that's my point is, is, you know, put on your detective hat, do more digging. Uh, something is bound to come up. It'll, it'll be a cascading process. Um, and there's a saying, you know, in copywriting that says, if you can't find a unique sales proposition or a value proposition, make one up. Don't, you know, don't make it, don't lie. You know, you might have to sort of go around it. And here I'll give an example. There was an investor who asked me to write copy for him and he was, that was the exact same situation. He was very dry, not a lot. And it was this, this technique, this unique technique that he discovered how to make money on the stock market. So I'm like, okay, is this is going to be your, just your standard, how to make money in the stock market, blah, blah, blah. Um, so I just got a conversation with him. I said, okay, you know, I'm done. Uh, let's just talk just for sake of talking. So how, you know, how's things. And he's talking about his wife and his kids and all that. And then he says, yeah, I have to go back for some more MRIs. What do you mean? MRIs? Yeah. Just don't make sure they're all clear because, you know, actually says that's the funniest thing. That's what got me to start the stock market. Years ago, uh, I found out I had a golf ball size tumor in my head and I couldn't work anymore. Um, I had it surgically removed. I'm fine now, but, um, I had this golf ball tumor removed and, and I had to find a way to make money because I couldn't go back to work. So I decided to do, to, you know, dabble in stock market. And that's exactly how I discovered this technique. Guess what the headline was for my sales letter after a golf ball size tumor removed from the Australian man's head, he discovers a secret to making money on the stock market. So it's sort of something like that, but basically it, it's, it's a, it's a, a point or thing that I just turned around and used that. And it did very well, by the way. And that's the point is that sometimes you have to manufacture. You don't lie. That was the letter was absolutely the 100% truth. But sometimes as a storyteller, you have to come up with the angle, the unique, you know, the unique value proposition. And sometimes it takes time, but it'll come if you do more research, if you have more of that detective work as that you were mentioning earlier. Have you ever changed the product so you could put something in the copy? Oh yeah. Um, excuse me. I had a, I had a client, I think it was about four or five years ago. He gave me this hue, like this whole package, uh, uh, to write copy with. And it wasn't selling really well. I mean, this is what I often do in copy nowadays is not writing brand new copy. It's actually rewriting existing copy that this is the convert or, or has pretty much, you know, petered out in terms of, of how many sales it can get. And, or saturated the market and, uh, or that's what they think. I looked at it and I thought, you know what, there is, first of all, you don't have any bonuses or any, your offer is good, but there's not anything that we can add to the product anymore. Um, so I said, how about you add this? How about you add that? Or how about we just take a few of these chapters, take it out because they're basically chapters that don't necessarily directly related to product, but we can take them out and make that, that's our own product. Put a value, an estimated value, price tag on it. And talk about how if you buy this, you'll also get this, which helps you to do that. And he literally re-engineered his product that way and sales went back up again. So I think as copywriters, we tend to do that. We have, you know, there's a, there's the, the famous, uh, um, debate about form versus function. Um, copywriters will either write based on what we have or we will write copy based on what we want to have. And I think that happens a lot in copywriting, especially if you're a good copywriter, you can see stuff. We are all, you know, entrepreneurs as well as copywriters. We can see what can actually sell better if we presented it differently. And sometimes we'll have to have clients, we do it. And, um, did I have clients in the past that refused to it? Yeah. Um, did it, did, did it do well? Some didn't do well. I had some, I wrote, I wrote copy for clients who were not, you know, amenable to changing stuff. And, um, my copy absolutely sucked. But then again, even if I change it, maybe it'll still suck. I'm not like, it's hard to predict, but you know, I know everybody has questions. So I'll, but I don't mean to, but that, this kind of dovetails into that. Um, cause I know you critique a lot of copy. I mean, that's, that's a great deal of what you do. Um, and in doing so, I know that you probably, you know, I mean, we could call it one of the biggest mistakes you see. That's a little surface low, but, but I think it. It bears talking about, but just also maybe at a little deeper level. I mean, just what, what are the things that you find, um, that have made the big difference? I mean, when you've got something that's it, like you say, it's a control that's been going and going and, and they're, they're about ready to just walk away. Cause it's like lost steam and you go on and critique it. What are the things you find that, um, whether it's mistakes people make, or just things that you think can be tweaked, changed, and, and breathe new life into this thing? I have, I, when I teach copywriting, one of the things that I do often is I use an mnemonics. Um, it's one of the things that I love doing, especially when I, it comes back to, you know, goes back to my, my, my college teaching days. And I love formulas for that reason, not because of, it's not because copies formulaic, I mean, it is to a degree, but it's also because it helps you to remember and helps you remember what you should do. And I developed the formula and I'm not new at this. Uh, this is something actually based on Eugene Schwartz break your advertising where he talks about the stage of, of market sophistication. And I developed a formula to explain that to clients. And, and let me answer your question first and I'll explain why. This is the biggest mistake that people make is that they misjudged the market. They often will think that their markets are in a particular, uh, level of awareness when they're not, and that's where they write copy. Like for the wrong, for the wrong market or for the wrong process of thinking in the market. And here's what I mean. I have a formula called oath and you can just say yourself, um, is your market are they ready to take an oath? Oath stands for O A T H oblivious, apathetic, thinking and hurting. So oblivious is they're oblivious about the problem. They don't even know they're suffering from the problem that you're actually solving with your, with your product or with your solution. Um, and, and the more oblivious your market is, that's when you need to, the longer the copy is, a lot of people say, how long should your copy is? It depends. Are the oblivious or the hurting, the more hurting your market is on the opposite end of that spectrum, you can just, you know, put a buy now button on your website and they'll buy that sucker because they're hurting like crazy. Um, but, but back to the point is that oblivious means, so they don't, they don't know they have a problem. You have to educate them about the problem. The next one is apathetic. So they know they have the problem, but they just don't, they don't care. They don't, they don't know if they don't really understand the importance of the problem. The next one is thinking. So they know the importance of the problem, but they're shopping around. So they're probably looking at alternatives, not, not just competitive, uh, products or services, but even other products or services that will help them solve that problem. Even sometimes they might be completely different. So you need to educate them a little bit less, but you need to educate them on why your product is better. So actually, let me go back oblivious. You have to educate them on the problem. Apathetic. You have to educate them on the importance of the problem. Thinking is you have to educate them on why your solution is best. And then hurting is it's pretty much, you know, you sell them on why they should buy now. The, it's more on the urgency. Uh, and so back to your question is what is the biggest mistake that I see copywriters make is they fail to judge the market properly. Where are they at in that stage of awareness? I'll read copy and I'll see that people are actually writing long, long, long copy because they think that their market is oblivious. When I find out through some research that there may be more in the thinking stage or opposite, they'll think that their market is hurting. I mean, everybody needs my product. That's a lot of clients say to me, but then you realize that a lot of them are oblivious or they're at least apathetic. And then now you, you have to sort of add more copy in order to educate that market. So that's where I think that the biggest problem is once that changes, because that also changes the, the what, you know, they say copywriting is, is two things. What to say and then how to say it. When I, when I do critiques, I often, I will do a critique based on how to say certain things. I'll look at a copy, I copy, which probably already does very well and I'll just help it to, to convert better. But sometimes I'll find copy where it's just the wrong angle. It's the wrong story or it's just the wrong level of awareness of the market. Then I, I, I, I hate to do critiques because those are very short critiques. I say, you just have to, you need a new angle, dude. And, and, and you know, I just, I know it's right. I know it's right. Yeah. It's like, I paint you 500 bucks, 500 dollars for that. So, so, yeah. So, and, and so the what to say, and that was my biggest epiphany in the last 10 years of copywriting, because up until John Reese's letter, it was easy for me to write copy on, on how to say certain things. I was good at that. I was good at rewarding stuff, coming up with different ways of saying the same thing. And, and you can jazz up, look, you know, a lot of people say, Fortnize, I want you to fortnize my copy. Yeah. And, and I would come up with just different ways of saying something that would hook people and that would be great. But sometimes if the, if, if the what is wrong, and this is where I found the biggest jump in conversions, especially when I'm doing split testing is not one sales letter with a red headline and one with a blue headline, or one with a completely different story angle or headline versus another. The Lord wildly different. They are the greatest. You can have double triple quintuple in conversion rates because of that. So here's my point is I realized that the what is so much more important than how the moment you have to what down, everything else is just gravy. Yeah. I think, I think we heard that from Mike Morgan. He did a launch and it was very, after all, it's worked a tiny fraction of what the control was doing. And that was exactly what it did. As he went back in and changed the angle on the shot all the way back up to that thing. And I don't think most of the copy body copy had changed. So yeah, it definitely was, you know, proves that the headline is 80% of the message. Just for what we're on that topic, Michael, if you've got, you mentioned that you would spend 80 to 90% of your time in research, but what is your process? So you've done your research and now you're going to sit down and write your sales page or video sales letter. Do you have a formulaic approach? Do you just start from the beginning to the end? How do you do it? You know, the very famous Ada formula, which is attention, interest, desire and action. I have a formula that I have sort of, it's based on that, but it applies more to a sales letter because the sales letter is a little bit different because Ada can be applied to ads, it can be applied to commercials, it can be applied to many different things. My formula that I use, and it's, it's, these are just guide posts. These are guide posts that help me to find out what I should need to research on. And it's called quest. Think about taking your client or your reader on a quest. Q U E S T qualify, understand, educate, stimulate and then transition. And here's what it means. Q, qualify. Well, a lot of people say, if my headline grabs their attention and gets them to start reading, it still won't sell if the person's not qualified. Whether you qualify them in the headline or a little bit later on, it doesn't matter. At least you have to qualify them in some way. They need to know when they read that headline or at least the, the, the, the, either the debt copy that they're, they're basically qualified for the offer. They are qualified for, for what is offered. And I say that because it's important. Well, a lot of people say, if I grab a headline that gets them to start reading, sometimes people use a little bit of intrigue in the headline and intrigue is perfectly fine, but then they fail. They say people read the headline, they start reading the copy, but they don't buy a lot based on, on, on stats. That's because you didn't qualify them properly. So qualify. I'll give you an example. A client of mine was a cosmetic surgeon who did facelifts and his ad used to say, do you have wrinkles? Question mark. And I thought, great. You're sort of qualifying your audience, but you're qualifying your audience based on who has wrinkles or not, but you know what, not a lot of, not everybody who has wrinkles wants to do something about it. So we changed the headline to suffering from wrinkles. Question mark and his ad response shot up dramatically because we properly qualified the reader. Now, the next one is you understand, and that's empathy. It's the single greatest tool in copy. One of my favorite copy writers, Brian Keith, Voils talks about when you write copy, you have to write it as if you are a blessing in the life of the reader at that moment. When the reader reads your copy, you are a blessing. That, that, that piece of copy is a blessing in their lives. And that's true. And, and so understand means when you write your copy, especially in the beginning parts of your copy, whether it's the lead or even further down in the copy, that they fully appreciate the problem that you understand the problem that you empathize with them and that you have something that will help alleviate their problem, their solution, their, their problems. So basically understand it's just empathy, applying empathy. And it's, it could be done with stories. Often it's done with stories. A lot of people will talk about, for example, if the owner of a product is selling a product based on his own story or her own story, usually telling that story will get people to connect with that because they say, I know what this person feels. I know exactly what they went through, et cetera. The next one is then educate. So now you, so now you qualify them, you understand their problem, then you educate them on the solution. And this is where you start describing your product. This is where you start talking about what it has, what it does, benefits and features and so on and so forth. And then stimuli, this is what they call in the, in the copy, the product introduction point, product introduction point. The next point is, is stimulate. So this is where you build value. This is where you add testimonials. You talk about, you know, risk reversal guarantees. You talk about the price, the price value, the price buildup. And you're getting to the point where you're actually making the offer. This is what stimulates me. This is where you get people excited about your offer. This is where you get them to, about to take action, whereas the next step, transition, that's transitioning them from a browser to a buyer, from a reader to a client. And I, you know, people say close and I use T because even though it's, it fits well with my formula, it's also a process that I love because I hate the word close. When people say close, that's the end of the relationship. Guess what? That's not the end of the relationship. The ordering process, even I, Gary was, Gary Halver was big on stick letters and writing good stick letters and educating people on how to consume the product to help reduce refunds and returns. Well, that process is all about transition. So all that to say, and it's, and transition a lot of times is also where a lot of, a lot of sales can be increased dramatically. And here's what I mean. David was asking me earlier about epiphanies when I do critiques. What are the biggest stumbling blocks? Very often if the, the headline, the copy, all those things are taken care of and they're done really well. The reason why people don't buy oftentimes is their speed bumps in the ordering process. Try to make that as smooth as possible. Try to remove those speed bumps. Uh, I, here's an example. Um, I remember four years ago, I was writing copy for a membership site in the, um, uh, I think it's the multimedia industry. And it was a membership site that actually trained people on how to create videos. And I don't think it was sales videos. It was just multimedia in general. And when people came to the ordering form, they, they were scared. They were like, okay, what exactly does that include? Now I know it includes the guy described it, but I'm going to be putting my credit card information. Uh, do I actually get training? Is it visual training? Or do I have to learn by writing? I mean, a lot of people say, well, I want, if I'm going to do videos, I want to know how to do videos with videos. And a lot of people didn't really know that. So basically at the ordering form, all we did was take a, a video of a tour of inside the site and we actually showed them, here's a video and that they'll show you how to do this. Here's a video that I'll show you how to do that. Once you get that video done, then you've got this other, uh, tips sheet that will be able to follow to make sure you all have all the technology in place and so on and so on and so forth. Or here's how to click to download your bonus or here's how to contact support. If you ever need any help where they're 24 seven, you can either email us or, you know, send a ticket or whatever the case is that one little video added to the order form tripled his conversion rate because we found out that the biggest sticking point was the ordering process. People were, you know, shopping cart abandonment as, as, as they say in e-commerce. Well, and this applies to any process in the ordering in the ordering process. Gary, how it often talks about adding a phone number to your order form. Sometimes that actually is what it's needed. Um, so taking away speed bumps, we'll, we'll just move that right along. So anyway, that's the transition part of the question from the quest Q, U, E, S, T. Fantastic. And do you think copywriters are, there's some people I know who are listening to this, who have just started the copyrighting career or who would like to become copywriters. Can anybody become a copywriter? I mean, is it a learned process or is there, is it a divine talent? What's your thoughts on that? I, here's my answer. And with, uh, with the caveat. Yes. Everybody, anybody can become a copywriter because I believe that anybody and everybody has a passion for something and or are, are, do persuade other people on things that they're interested in, whether it's an idea, whether it's a product or service, whether it's a brand, whether it's a, it's a store. It doesn't matter. I know that if I were to talk to somebody who believes they're not a writer, but if they sit down at a, you know, have a beer at the restaurant and then you talk to me about this movie they just saw and how excited that movie was. I'm like, Oh my gosh, you should see the new transformer movie or whatever. So that is sales, it's salesmanship. It's, it's being excited about something. The only process, the only stumbling block or the only not, not, not something but the only hurdle that you have to overcome is being able to put that on paper. So can you be a copywriter? Absolutely. But sometimes you will have to have either an editor help you out because sometimes you can have all these ideas on paper and you just need somebody to help manage those ideas around or make sure there's a proper flow. Well, guess what? You are a copywriter, not a copy editor. You can, you know, there's a, there's a famous saying by Peter Stowe and actually the gentleman that you see on your screen who, who was the, the the concierge for our, our, our conversation that I had with Gary Halpert and John Carleton, he said this, it was brilliant point. He says, write fearlessly, but then edit ruthlessly, you know, or, or write first and edit later. And I think that's oftentimes the stumbling block for copywriters is that they're very critical. They stop halfway and then basically it stifles a creative process. Don't just, let it come out. Just write, write, write. If you have to dictate, I do not want to be a copywriter. I do a lot of copy right now by dictating. Do you know how Siri has gotten better? You know, I use my, it's, it's really fantastic. And no, and here's another thing. I write copy in French now. I actually have French clients from Montreal and Siri now has, you know, I can change from French to English and it'll, it'll dictate pro, you know, perfectly. Um, it's not as bad as it used to be. Actually, it'll, it'll, if I, if I'm in a very quiet room, it'll capture everything I say properly. If I'm in a very busy place, sometimes I try to do this at Starbucks. And you like autocorrect everything. It's like, I can't understand what they're saying, but, but I dictate a lot of stuff. And then I bring it back home and I, and I sort of move things around. I use those formulas that I talked about, or I'll have somebody else take a look at and say, what do you think? Or do you think I should talk about this here? Do you think I should put that there? Um, here are about, here are 20 different headlines. Which one do you think grabs you the most? And so on and so forth. So, so to answer your question, ultimately, I believe everybody has it. Um, can you, can you, do you have it innately? Yes. But can you, does it, does it come naturally? Hmm. That's where probably you need, you need training. You need to have some, at least a foundational skillset in salesmanship. Or at the very least be able to express your ideas. If, you know, worst case scenario, worst case scenario, if you're selling something that you're passionate about, and sometimes that's where clients come in, um, you probably need to be interviewed by somebody who will be able to extract those ideas out of you and write copy with that. It still makes them a copywriter in a sense. It's just not the, the, the copyrighting process that most people think about. And very often that's how a lot of people will write great copy. Um, I, I know, I know, for example, on John Rees, um, when he writes copy, and I've seen him do this for other products that I actually helped him on. I wrote the traffic secrets, but I also helped him on other copy that he wrote. Excuse me. And I actually saw him do it live. Um, you know, when you're passionate about it, it just comes out. I mean, it wasn't like, it wasn't, there was no rhyme or reasons what he's saying, but it just came out and not gets a goal was just coming out on screen like crazy. And then, and then after that I was, he says, okay, Michael, just, you know, make it, make it read properly and, and put it together in the proper form. And then I would do that. Um, but that's, and, and, and John is very geeky. And a lot of people say, well, John is a great copyright, but you know what? And he, you know, he, he, that isn't think that he's a great copywriter. He's, he believes more that he's a geek, but all that being said, I think that everybody has that ability. It's just that it, the process might be different. If, if you were to ask me, what would be the single biggest thing that people need to do in order to learn how to write copy? Just learn selling. I remember when I first started in sales, I've listened to every single sales trainer in the world. My car, I mean, I remember the days of tape decks. Um, uh, my car, I had, my, my truck was filled with Dan Kennedy tapes with Tom Hopkins, Zig Ziglar, uh, Brian Tracy, uh, um, Roger, Roger Dawson, uh, power negotiating for salespeople. Um, gosh, I mean, I got, I got tapes galore and I listened to everything. And I got good at selling. My problem was getting people to, to, to book an appointment. And that's where copyright came in. But that's my point is that salesmanship in print is truly, it's, it's truly, uh, a powerful statement that people need to grasp more if they want to know how to write copy. I say, don't write copy, learn how to sell. Then you'll be able to write copy because basically you can choose the medium or the process and how you write copy. That's different. That's, that's, that's individual and it can be different for everybody. You can dictate, you can have somebody interview. It doesn't matter, but learn how to sell and then you'll be able to write copy. Great answer. And, uh, what was your, what was your favorite Gary story? My favorite, what? Gary, Gary Halper story. Gary Halper. I don't think this podcast is, uh, far away. Let me, let me, let me, let me, let me give you one. We can edit it. Well, well, not only that, they'd love to hear it. So I'm going to give you a little bit of a salacious one. Don't worry. Well, I can tell you this is that if Gary, like I said, Gary wanted to make a point, he would tell the story and he would drive the point home. It just remarkably well. And, uh, I remember one time he called me up because he wanted my opinion on some other person that, uh, he was looking at doing business with that I knew. And, uh, I gave him my opinion and, and he says, just something about him that I'm not sure what it is. And then he would tell a story of something that happened to him to explain how he feels about him. That's that's that's Gary Halper. And that story is a little R rated. So I won't tell you that story, but let me tell you another one. I remember doing a seminar with him and John Carleton and we were at a hotel and I just arrived and John was already there and we were at the bar and then we said, listen, uh, Gary's here. Uh, let's just go up and see him so we can actually talk about the schedule tomorrow. And so we go upstairs and he had like a pen out sweet. I can't remember what it was. And we're knocking at the door. We don't say who we are. He opens the door naked. And John, John says, Jesus Christ, Gary get dressed. And he just looks at me and he looks at me because he knows, I know I'm not John and there's somebody else in and he says, Oh, okay. Um, and, and funny thing is that we went into the room. We taught, we, he had like the books laid out and all the things that we wanted to talk about tomorrow. We actually had products that we wanted to sell and, and, and all that stuff. And the whole meeting just kept being done with him pretty much in his, in his, uh, his tiny whiteies, you know, that was, that was, uh, that's Gary. And that's about all I can say that could be. Yeah. You know, on that same, uh, note though, um, and I know it, it probably maybe something we've touched on and covered, but you know, what, what do you think? Cause you had a lot of, um, a lot of conversation, a lot of time with, with Gary that, you know, those that did are very fortunate for having that. What do you think is the single most important thing you ever learned from Gary Halbert? Um, well, what I just mentioned, which is the power of metaphors, the power of thinking in relative terms, and sometimes I still struggle with that to this day. I'm 47 and I've been running copy for 25 years and I still have sometimes struggling with what I want to say that people can understand and relate to properly. Um, I, I, again, we've talked about acronyms and, uh, mnemonics. Uh, I have one called upwards UPWRDS universal picture words or relatable descriptive sentences. Again, just an analogy, but or metaphor or, or story. And, and I think that's the one thing that I learned about Gary is that he was doing it and it was so natural to him because he was a natural storyteller, store seller. Um, and I would love to be more like him. And I think that's one of the reasons why every time a Gary Halbert newsletter would come out, um, or a new product would come out or an interview I had with him is the stories and, and, and it wasn't just the stories, but his ability to also impress on you, the power of stories, because he, part of his teaching was about the power of telling stories. And I think that was my biggest, that was my biggest change in the last, I think the 12 years that I've known him 10, 10, 12 years that I've known him is the ability to learn how to tell good stories and make a point that not just people can walk away with and say, Oh, that was an interesting point. It's also a point that changes their minds, that gets into take action, that gets them to understand what you're trying to sell and what you're all about. Um, Gary was phenomenal at doing that. And, uh, if, if there's anything that I still need to work on, that would be it. And it's still, it's, it's interesting because we've talked, you know, so much about that in halbertology. And I think it, because of its importance, obviously, but I, I think it is one of the hardest things to learn how to do. If you're not born with it, you know, some people are just natural storytellers. They're, they're hard to shut up. Uh, and, and they engage people and they, you know, um, other people, it's very difficult. It's just not something you were born with or, or, you know, the way you were raised, it just didn't, you weren't around it or whatever. And it's very difficult to learn that skill. Yeah. I think when, when you just don't have it, but, but, but here's the thing though. Don't think of it as a skill. So, I mean, it is a skill, but don't think of it as a skill. If you're trying to learn it, try to look at it from, if you have to look at it from a scientific standpoint, look at what's the end point. What is the moral of the story? What is exactly do you want to do or what you want your audience to do? And then look for either stories or a way to tell something that would get them to understand that point relatively. A great example of that, I'm sure you guys will remember Paul Harvey and the rest of the story. Oh yeah. Um, and that was amazing, wasn't it? And that's sort of like copy because he would tell the story, this unknown story about somebody who you think, Oh my gosh, this, who is this person? And then you realize that there was this famous person all along or what have the case was, but you see, it started with the end in mind. And I think that we need to do that as copy orders more. Um, a lot of people start and, and that's great to start. I'm not saying that that's wrong. I'm just saying that if you have a hard time, go to the end and then come back. If you have to make a point to your client about the importance of X, Y, Z of your product, well, what story can actually relate that to the client? Or can you make a story or are there stories online? You can do some research and I'm sure there's stories online that you can find out that we'll be able to tell that, that, that, that drive that point home. Um, or is there a story that you saw that you can either credit or rearrange that it's your own story, uh, not based on, not something that is actually historical and I don't want, you know, copyrighted to lie. That's not my point. My point is you can tell a story in a hypothetical sense. I think it was bought. I was talking about hypothetical stuff. That's a great way of telling a good story that gets people, you know, their attention and get them to take action. So some of the end in mind, it is a skill and it is not easy. Again, I still struggle with it, but like I said, learning, reading fiction, you know, Stephen King's on writing. Um, another book that I highly recommend is, uh, Breakthrough Advertising by Eugene Schwartz, um, because he talks about that as well. Just be able to go back and look at what you exactly do. You want your market to do and then see what exactly would get them to move. If there's one particular point that'll get them moving, don't just say that point, tell a story that will drive that point home. Because people, if you say, if I say something that I, if I talk about, like, I'm looking at my computer right now and I'll give you one major point about my computer, that may or may not get you interested. At least it won't make you remember because you're probably just, you know, okay, thank you. But if I tell you a story that has that point included, you're going to remember it. You're going to be, you know, uh, hopefully driven to buy that product because of that story. So, and plus the power of empathy. Remember I talked about the chorus formula. When you're telling that story, it adds that in there. There's all those elements, qualify, understand, educate, stimulate, transition. You can all do all that with, with stories and you can actually drive that point home, uh, far more powerfully that way. I think it's interesting that you'd like Paul Harvey, my dad lived for that guy. Oh, go ahead, Bob. Is that Bob? Um, yeah. Um, what I wanted to mention was one of the, yeah, Kevin and I grew up listening to Paul Harvey. We had Paul Harvey time all the time. And, um, he really loved also the ads and stuff in there because Paul Harvey would have to invite you to do your ads. Yeah. He would not, uh, you couldn't just go and say, I want to add on you. What he would do is he would say, Hey, listen, I like your product. Would you like to run an ad on my show? And it was great. Um, but anyway, the, there was, uh, I got a tactical question. I just want to reinforce one point that we were making earlier. You know, a lot of people don't know this, but our father's stories were very rehearsed and Kevin and I are one of the few people to see him experience something that was later to become a story and then see him rehearse it and share it over and over again, over years in time. You know, we all talk about, well, we don't all talk about it, but we, there's always the story about my dad getting arrested for having sex with a cow. And that, you know, I wasn't there for that one, fortunately, because that was when he was in the military. Um, and he just, to stop everybody, he was innocent. He didn't do anything like that. It's a hilarious story though. It is, but the, but the point is he, he rehearsed that story in person over time. And so I think that if somebody, if you have a good story, I think it's important for people to know that it is something that evolves. It's just like when you tell a story about something that happened in high school, you innately start to learn, this is the dry part to drop out. And this is the part that excites people to save the big bang for the end. And so I don't, I don't want people to think that, you know, if you're, you're born a natural storyteller or you're not, because here's one of the greatest storytellers and he's practicing. But my tactical question and come, come back because we've never really covered it with how vertology, but it's a very big and powerful part of our dad's, uh, power, which is stick letters that you mentioned. And it's a slightly different because you're not as worried about grabbing attention, you know, you've already, you know, once you've given somebody, somebody's giving you their credit card, you've got their attention. Right. You know, because they're like, Hey, you got my money. So what elements do you find are good to put into a good stick program? And just for everybody who doesn't know a stick program is, um, what you're going to do after the sale to help encourage people to be happy with the product and to stick with it and to not refund or return it. And so I just, you know, since you brought it up and that's a great, it's a great, you know, what do you try and put into a good stick letter? Three things. Educate them on how to consume the product. I think that's number one key. A lot of people don't necessarily know, or they think it's intuitive. You know, a lot of clients say, Oh, I don't want to write a stick. They know how to reuse my product. It's intuitive. It's the same thing with copy. A lot of people write copy thinking, Oh, I shouldn't, you know, I shouldn't write this part. They, they know what it means or they know what it does or they know about that. Whatever. We tend to always assume, uh, what we think our market understands or will intuitively know, but the oftentimes, you know, there's no saying that people are, are, um, the leader lives of quite desperation. They want to be led. They want to be told what to do, uh, even though they might not necessarily admit that, um, and that's true of copy as well as stick letters is that you, you need to sort of take them by the hand, explain to them how to consume the product. Because that removes so many, not only problems on, uh, um, tech support or even customer support after the fact, but also a lot of people who do ask for a refund or return will often do it just because they fail to understand how to use the product properly. So the stick letter can involve that. The second is the ability to have communication lines open, making sure that they know where they can get supported. They need to. So very often people will think once I buy the product, they're just, that's it. I'm gone. Uh, you need to tell them, reinforce the idea that I'm there all the way. I'm not just there just to make the sale. I'm there for you to actually help enjoy the product and the benefits of the product. And Gary was powerful at telling stories that would do that. And even though the point is just to say that, Hey, uh, uh, you know, we're here. We're just a phone call away or a word, just an email away. And the third oftentimes is I add something that's extra that they didn't even expect a mystery bonus, an unexpected bonus, something that was not part of the sales process or the sales letter, because that reinforces a, the gift by mentality, but B they think, wow, they're, you know, they're, they're taking part of, they're, they're helping me that they're, they're taking care of me. Um, they're adding me in this, they give me this extra thing and it helps you reduce refunds. You know, Dan Kennedy used to call them refund killers because once you give them a gift, they think, well, if I, if I ask for a refund, I have to give back this gift. So very often, uh, people will just keep the product for that reason. But, but again, it's not, I'm not trying to say that for that reason. I'm just saying that those are the three things that I have to make sure that my stick letters have. And you can, like I said, you can use stories to do that. But oftentimes, uh, Gary would be a master at, at writing stick letters that would actually tell stories that would drive all those plowants home in some way. Um, and I think also at the same time, you use a little bit of that, uh, intrigue. Uh, I talked a little bit about this during the call, but, you know, when we say intrigue, a lot of times people, what do people love the most? They love cliffhangers because people hate being left on a cliff. They really love closure and they love cliffhangers because it's, it's a sense of excitement because after like, you know, once we found out who shot JR, Dallas ratings went down, right? Well, it's the same idea with copy as much as it is with stick letters. Sometimes I remember Gary would write a stick letter and he would always say, you know, I'm going to tell you about how to do blah, blah, blah, but only the next letter and or, or, or only in the next or only in the product, which is forcing people to consume the product. So intrigue is very powerful in stick letters as much as it is in copy. Um, you know, it's, it's sort of like that serialized process that, that you kept people on that shoot and it doesn't necessarily mean on one piece of copy. And a lot of times people say, uh, long copy, everything has to be done in one big, long sales letter. Sometimes it can be done through a process, through multiple steps and just make sure that you keep people hanging on every single step. And stick letters are included in there. So great. Okay. I'm going to ask you another question just cause I, but now go ahead, but on time. Okay. No, I just, I've been kind of quiet. So I just want to ask another question. Um, you mentioned earlier about getting to know the product and empathy. And I think one of the things that you're also saying, but it's not said when you say product is to know their experience. It's to knowing what they feel and what they're going through. And our father's biggest breakthrough moment that started his career was to understand as the famous APAL, B-PAL speech. If you, if anybody's never heard that, just go look it up on our sites and stuff like that. Uh, he really, what he did was he stopped and he said, okay, wait a minute. This is what it's like for some guy who's come home from a hard day of work to just come home to a mountain of junk mail and start sifting through it and doing this and that. And I reverse engineered that and actually just applied that to email marketing because I realized that the way people consume their emails different from the way that they go through their physical mail. And you alluded to this earlier because social media is, you know, the, the mindset of somebody on Twitter is actually kind of different than the mindset of somebody who's on Facebook. You're in there for a different purpose, for a different reason. And I'm just going to take a very small, quick tangent because I mentioned it earlier, you know, Kevin and I sat down, I remember very vividly showing our dad in a computer in a small office that the worldwide web and how we thought it was going to be just this revolutionary thing. And our father just would not believe it. He just like, no, it's a, it's a glorified filing system. It doesn't make a difference. You know, that's all nonsense. But when he finally took hold and said, okay, well, this is going to be good. I'm going to try this and I'm going to try to experiment. I noticed because I was putting together something with my kids because I'm trying to get them interested. And we started going back and discovered all of our father's emails for the Gary Halber letter, all of his emails saying, Hey, come read my newsletter. And what was amazing about it that I realized is what he was really doing was he went through that process. He understood it so well of what you would experience that he would start to mitigate the problems. He would start to, you know, to give you clear explanations to how to smooth out the home and it evolved over time because the first one started off with being sure that you knew how to clearly spell and put that URL into the, into the, to the address bar, which nobody would even bother with today. You know what I'm saying? Because, and then, you know, you saw it, you know, and he had this curiosity and stuff that he was driving to make you have to go check out his letter. And going with what you were talking about, leaving you with that cliff anger saying, and I'm going to teach you this in your next letter. He even confided in me and others. He said, you know, the truth is I don't necessarily know what I'm going to, you know, I wrote that PS because I was in the mood and I was in the, I was in the motions and he didn't, and he's like, but bonk, I'll always be able to deliver it the next time. So he, he would actually say, and next week I'm going to show you how to, you know, increase your sales 1000%. He had no clue what he was actually going to do. But I know this all leads to the question that I have for you, which is, what do you think about the changing and it's different, different for different mediums, but what do you think about the change in attention span that's going on? Because I see, you know, and I know this sometimes affects people with long versus short copy, but I also think it affects their ability to perform what we call the so wet test, that ability to go through this, imagining that you really, you don't know who Michelle Fortner, Bonn-Halbert or Kevin Halbert is, and you're just a busy person who has a problem and you just want them to get right to the points of what matters to me. That's, you know, something titillating or it could be, you know, super beneficial. But what do you think about the current state of attention span and, you know, the, you know, being able to fully understand the experience that everybody's going through when you're, as David would say, not, you know, you're not trying to be too close to it. You know, do you have any tips on that? Or do you have any comments on, you know, where it's changed over the years? Well, of course it has. And that was part of the process of why I wrote the death of the sales letter, because it's not just attention spans or how people consume information. It's also the frame of mind that people are in. I mean, if you read an article in newspaper versus reading it on your mobile phone, that's two different states of mind. That's two different frames of mind. So this is where, as a copywriter, it's the job is even harder nowadays, because now you have to distill as much as you possibly can to the most essential, you know, eyeball grabbing elements that you didn't have before when you had the ability to say more. And that's probably where it's hard. A lot of people say, oh, writing long copies are no writing short copies harder, because you really need to know what you need to bring down to the core essence of what will get people's attention. So oftentimes using intrigue, using serialized processes can often give you the ability to tell the full story, but in the chunk, chunkified process. What I've been doing with, there's a particular client that I know who actually is exactly like that. He used to have long copy sales letters. He would have videos. And you see this also in launch formulas now, big, big launches with new internet marketing products, where it's no longer just a big long sales letter, it's like a small little video and then stay tuned. Video number two is coming next week and then they never tune in. Number three is coming in after that is maybe a teaser and then after that is a funny full launch. Well, it keeps people in that loop, right? So rather than giving them head on, and of course it's all about creating anticipation, I understand that. But that's where we probably need to use more anticipation because of the fact that people can be so distracted, so easily with internet and mobile, especially mobile computing these days. So I do that with a client where we used to have a copy, long copy in almost all of his products, but now he's using the spoon feeding method where we'll basically chunkify parts of the copy and serialize it over either a repeat of time or if they want to, they can consume the information on the spot, but they will actually, and that's the dynamic part, that's the kinesthetic part I was talking about earlier, where they're involved in the copyrighting process, in the copy reading process. So they're telling you what they want. Sometimes it's not really that proper time where they can read the whole thing. So they'll either go back to it at their laptop or wait till that at a better time. So the frame of mind too, and again coming back to the frame of mind thing. One of the things that I often tell people is when you write copy, try to picture in your mind, and if you have to, put it on the wall somewhere, a picture of that ideal customer, the perfect prospect profile. A lot of people will call it, you know, the biopersona. Well, I call it the perfect prospect profile. And basically it's tell a story, you can tell a story of a day in the life of that prospect. So that sort of underlines what you just said, Bonne. And I talk about, for example, there are four typical things that we do market research on, demographic, psychographics, geographics and technographics. So demographics are characteristics and qualities of your market. Psychographics are buyer characteristics or psychology or buying history. Hobbies that they're interested in, what makes the person emotionally with who they are. And then third is geographies where they're located, if they're using, if they buy it from work or if they buy it home, if they buy in different countries or different states, whatever the case is. And then finally, technographics, which used to be said about people who are technophobic or technophiles, meaning are they averse to technology or do they adopt technology easily? And I think that pretty much everybody nowadays use technology in some way. So now the question is, do they have the tendency to buy or surf the internet on their mobile phones or their tablets or on their computers at home? And if there's a computer at home, is it a laptop or is it desktop? So those are just elements that you try to find out by your client. And if you do market research, you'll find all that stuff. But now tell a story. Tell a story of a day in the life so that when you write that copy, you become, like I said, Brian Kievoel said before, a blessing in that, and that at that particular time in their lives, at that particular time in their day, and how would you say that? Or how would you do that so that they would stop and say, oh, I've got to read this now. And then once they're done, like, where has the time been? So develop that buyer persona, keep it in your mind and put it on. I sometimes, what I do is I'll actually write bullet points. I'll actually have a pad and paper pencil and I'll write down bullet points of what that person, what makes up that person. And I'll put it on my desk right above me. And when I'm writing copy, sometimes I'll go in a tangent and I realize, oh, this is not important to that person because they don't do that or they do this. So I'll go back and I'll change or edit it. So understanding that client, especially the frame of mind. So what keeps them up at night? What makes them preoccupied? What keeps them, what makes them happy? What makes them excited? If they were to buy this product and be excited and tell their friends about it, how would they say it? What exactly would they be excited about? What would be the one thing? So they're hot buttons. And a lot of people say, don't talk about sex, politics or religion and copy. But those are sometimes the things that get people aroused. People were talking like crazy. Geez, I know there's a Gary Helberg joke in there somewhere. Talk about sex and aroused. But the point is, is that trying to get into the mind, crawling into the mind of your client so that when you write, you're not writing yourself for the product or for your client, you're a copywriter. You're writing for the market. So that when we, you know, when I used to teach Algonquin College, there's a process where we talk about advertising or copywriting is a process of encoding the message so that it'll be decoded by the client and then they can act upon it, like the sender encoding the message, decoding and then receiver. That's fine and all that. But people have to understand that the process of a copywriter is to be able to to encode the message in a way that will be decoded the way that you want them to decode it once they receive that message. We can encode, we can tell sales letter, we can tell story in many different ways, millions of different ways. It doesn't necessarily mean that it'll be understood or appreciated or acted upon the same way that we think. So we need to sort of step out of our own shoes. There's an old saying that says, before I walk a mile in your shoes, I must first take off my own. As copywriter, we need to do that. We need to sort of walk, take out, get out of our own skin, get it into the minds of the prospect and understanding what it makes them tick, what makes them riled up. You know, even if it's sometimes those are hot topics, you might want to avoid that in a copy, but at least you understand what their mindset is when you write that copy, right? So that's what I think is important. You're muted again, Fon. Real quickly, and I'll let everybody, oh, there you go. It says I'm unmuted. Am I muted or okay? No, I'm going to say this last, this will be my last question before I let everybody else ask more questions.
