Just a small little side story. I was at a seminar, I spoke on copy at a seminar in Toronto about a month ago. And one of the speakers, and I can't remember the tool, but there's a, there's a, there's a program online. I think it's called Buddy Bear or something like that. And it's, it's basically you log in and you're able to, it's, it's like these teddy bears, right? But you're able to dress up your teddy bear the any way you want, give it a story, give it a background. Um, and, and then when it, when it does it, it spits out this persona of this bear. And I, and I kind of laughed at that, but I said, you know what? If as a copy or sometimes that's a great tool, you know, describe your client and this, well, how are they dressed? Um, what do they do in the morning? What kind of breakfast do they eat? Are they vegan or are they paleo enthusiasts? And all that stuff. So, um, that would be actually a cool tool. You know, because we edit copy that's going on mobile phones on a desktop. We're not actually seeing it the way it is until we put it through the preview thing and get response or something. Yeah. You know, and I really think that makes a huge, huge difference in understanding that because, you know, when I started figuring out early before mobile revolution, uh, or still mobile revolution, but, you know, how people were giving out fake email addresses, spam email addresses to people they were unsure of. I was going through the same empathetic process you're talking about that our dad explained. And that was, you know, and that's, that's the story that I mentioned about him. His emails evolving over time. It's because he understood getting on his computer and worrying about getting lost in the early days of the internet. And stuff like that. I think, I think it would be an extremely kind of helpful tool to help keep, you know, keep those key aspects in mind and help you deal with the fact that the mediums are different. You know, I mean, everybody is looking in like a newspaper, you're reading the articles, you're looking for a place to feel fully informed and comfortable to stop reading. But so I'll work with that. If you want to create it, that'd be awesome. There's, there's a website, um, called which test one dot, I believe it's which test one dot com that site. And this morning, the test that came out this morning was awesome. Basically it was, uh, version a was this long scrolling copy with a lot of credibility, testimonials, guarantee. Uh, and it's just getting people to sign up to a list. And then the second version was this compact version that would fit on mobile device, not just mobile, the other one would fit on mobile devices as well, but it would be scrolling. But the second one was very short and sweet with one major point that would invite them to subscribe. Then it was just at one point, all the credibility elements were taken off. And a lot of people say, Oh, credibility, that should, that's the one that's actually going to sell more. No, guess what version be outsold, you know, buy two to one. Um, and that's, that's mobile computing because people now more and more, you know, you talk about spam email addresses and all that. Um, but people are becoming more, uh, they're warming up to, to buy them on mobile devices more and more. So we don't need as much credibility elements that we used to. Uh, however, that's part of the knowing your audience, because like I said before, I will copy for, you know, uh, senior men who play golf and that's totally different than your, you know, teenage kid on, on, uh, you know, who skateboards. So if your picture of the golfing guy was sitting there with his newspaper in the morning, it's going to help you reinforce. Yeah, exactly. I have two questions. One of them is kind of for the newbie process thing, uh, which is when you're crafting a piece of copy, be it a sales letter or a sales page, how long do you generally, you do you generally spend? I know you don't do a lot of it now, but when you were churning out brand new copy, how long would you take? Well, again, the, the, the question of the, the percentages that I mentioned earlier, 80% was spent on research, 10% was spent on copies. So if I was given it, let's say, um, a month to write copy, the first two to three weeks, if not more would be spent on doing a lot of research. Right. And in one week I would turn out that copy sometimes in days. Heck, I remember for John, Mrs. traffic secrets, I actually, I, I, I for the three days prior to the launch, I didn't sleep at all. Um, because, and, you know, why? Because when I wrote the first sales letter, he actually John Reese said, no, no, I don't like it. I had to actually rewrite the sales letter, but that's because I didn't understand the market as much as John did. And John gave me some nuggets of gold. And then I rewrote and look, it made a million dollars in one day. Right. So, um, again, it, it, it, how long? I mean, it's, if you're a brand new writer, sometimes you're writing your brand new writer, sometimes you might have to take more than that. So give yourself some wiggle room, do a lot of research, of course, but when you write copy, also give yourself a chance to maybe come back and change things because that's what I didn't know, especially when you're hitting a deadline. I can write copy and hit a deadline. Doesn't this mean it's the right copy? So make sure that you have, go, go back and, you know, this is more of a time management thing, but look at your deadline and come back and say, how much time do I need in order to, in order to finalize a final draft, then the first draft, then the rough draft, then no research. And then you can look at that and say, well, that will take me X number of time a month, a month and a half, whatever. Okay. Thank you. Uh, the other thing is you went through your, um, upwards mnemonic really fast and Mary in our audience was like, can he repeat that please? Upwards means, um, universal picture words. And I'll explain that with that means universal picture words or relatable, descriptive sentences. Now picture words is just using words that help to create vivid mental imagery. You know, John Carlton says, don't just, don't, don't beat your competition. You only got to murder your competition. You know? You know, that's, that's what universal picture words means. And universal means that it's understood universally because a lot of people sometimes will deal with words that will only be appreciated by a portion of the market or it's like some inside thing. You have to be careful. So use picture words that people can understand, murder. Everybody can understand what the word murder means. And that's pretty strong. And relatable descriptive sentences are metaphors and analogies, um, um, stories, allegory, you know, all those things. Um, and sometimes we need to sort of look at copy and take what we wrote and translate that or, or, or codified into a story, even though we might already have that in our copy. Here's why I mean, uh, great example of this. Um, there was a commercial that I saw on it's summertime right now and there's a commercial, a public service commercial telling kids to be careful. If you see a bear in the woods, you know, we, we do get bears here in the woods here near, uh, Ottawa, Ontario. And what to do. And they say kids, if you see a bear, please remain about a hundred feet away. Does a child know what a hundred feet means? So, so the commercial said, and this is where the brilliant was, if you see a bear, make sure to stay at least two bus lengths away or two or three bus links away, whatever that, that means. And of course my math might be wrong, but that, but the example remains. And, and that was the point is that sometimes when we write copy, we kind of think that our clients will understand what we're saying. And, and, and this, this whole, uh, interview was based on the fact that Gary is a great storyteller. Well, that's because Gary knew how to tell a story, not just to say the story, but to get people to understand what the story meant. And that's, that's sometimes it's not natural or not quick. I mean, frame it was natural, but sometimes you just need to reread your copy and see if there's any stomach blocks, if there's any speed bumps in your copy, sometimes it's just something that people don't, don't grasp. If I tell you, for example, um, think of a garbage can, are you going to think G, a, R, B, eight? No, you're not going to, you're going to visualize a garbage can and you're going to even do better job. If I, if I do that visualization for you, I actually take you by the hand. I say, think of a green plastic, disgusting garbage can with, uh, that's, you know, that with Oscar the grouch in it, you know, so I'm describing it. And that's, that's what relatable descriptive sentences describe it. Tell a story, talk about, you know, ways that people can grasp what you're talking about. So that's what upwards means. Great. Thank you so much. Cause that was, I was trying to get it down. So I type these things into the chat for the members and I couldn't get it down. So thank you. I was really glad Mary asked for that. Uh, well guys, we are coming up on the two hour mark. So let me ask, is anybody have any other burning question or does Michael have anything he's dying to share? Yeah. One last question just for the, um, for the newbies, the, for the people who want to become copywriters. And I know we spoke about whether they were sort of, uh, born or made, but for somebody starting off in their career, uh, what, one piece of advice would you give them? Other than learning how to sell. Hmm. Uh, well, the one thing that I love that Gary teaches and it's the thing I've done and it's still, and I'll do it with a typewriter. Although Gary says, oh, you got to do it by hand because when you write a salesman by hand, there's a connection between your hand and your brain that, that you cannot do whenever you're writing on, on a keyboard. I kind of say, yeah, that's true. And I used to write my hand all the time, but now I write by keyboard. But here's the thing, rewrite existing winning salesletters word for word by hand, if you have to, or if you have to just use a keyboard. Um, what I would, what I did when I first started out is I had multiple screens on my computer. I would have a laptop and then I'd have two screens sub by side. I use that in order to sometimes when I write copy, I have a screen open with, uh, uh, some website of, of a client or some research that I'm doing. And then there's the, the questionnaire on another screen. And then I have my, my copy that actually write on my, my main laptop. Um, what I would do is I would take an existing sales that are done. I knew did very, very well. And I would literally take my, my, my, you know, I'll take my keyboard and literally write out the entire sales that are word for word without skipping a word, without missing a beat. And that alone was life changing for me because then I understood the thought process that went into the person who wrote that sales letter. Very often I don't think of the market. I don't think of, of, I think of the market, of course, but that's not my point. My point is I think of when a person wrote that letter in the first place, why would they, why would they say this? How were they thinking? And oftentimes that makes me understand more about the market because it makes me understand how the copy were thought about the market. Why did he put this thing? Why did he talk, I don't, oh, and then you, and it just clicks. You do it several times and several times. And so this is my number one tip. It's not mine. It's Gary Helbert's tip. Rewrite existing winning sales letters. Type it if you have to, by hand, if you can, that's the way to do it. Fantastic. Anybody else? No, I just, uh, the only thing I wanted to talk about was just to mention, um, that you talked about the, the resistance to pay and make the sales and stuff like that. I just have this strong gut feeling that Apple pay is going to explode our entire market. I don't know why, but I just, you know, uh, I don't know. It just, uh, I think it's going to change everything for us. Um, and it's just something you triggered in my brain. So I don't know why. It's funny because, you know, there's a saying in marketing that says that every major industry is always a two horse race. Um, John Reese and, uh, sorry, um, uh, Al Reese and Jack Trout wrote the book, um, positioning the bell for your mind and wrote another book called the 22 immutable laws of marketing. And in one of the laws, it was the fact that every major industry is led by two horse race. So you have Pepsi and Coke, uh, you know, Ford and chef, whatever the case is. So in the checkout or in the payment industry, uh, Apple pay, I think PayPal has been the leader in that industry for so, so long. Google checkout tried and they failed. Um, I'm not sure exactly what's happening with them now at this point, but I think that we need another major player and I think that's, that's going to be it, it might be it. Um, I'm not sure, but that might be the case. Is that, is that what you're talking about? Apple pay? Yeah. Apple pay. I just think I don't, I know it's kind of, kind of outside of having you as an expert, but just cool. Okay. No, it's just, it's just the idea that I think that, you know, I know people are talking about paying with RFC, where you just, you know, wave your phone at things and you buy a Coke or you pay your bill or do all this other stuff and all that security, I think that sense of security that there's, um, that is simple. You can just make an impulse move and no one has to learn how to pay for anything ever again, enter in all their personal info, worry about a secured network. And, um, um, I think it's, I think it's just going to be a game changer. I think everyone's marketing is going to pretty much say, stay the same, but that taking away that, that barrier, that speed bump you talk about, I think is going to, um, take everything that's, even if it's sitting existing, if they can just swap out, you know, PayPal has certain advantages, you know, uh, upsell funnels or a, um, stuff like that. But if, if Apple does that, I just think it's, it's going to, um, it's going to rush their money. Yeah. Yeah. Cause I, you know, it's just like my psych security is up and I try to tell everybody, Hey, we don't even take your money on our website. Even if our psych security certificate's gone, you just go to PayPal and you're paying there anyways. So that's where the whole thing is. But I think you want to hear a small story. Uh, I just, I love messenger bags, which is, you know, a lot of people say it's a most male person. I love them. I buy, and I have mine. It's so you knew it's right here, you know, um, but I, and I was, I was shopping for a new one because my, the one that had before, um, decided to, to die on me. And every single messenger bag at the bag store had RF protection technology. It's, it's such a big thing now. And I didn't know this. And then I, and I remember, I remember reading about how people got their credit cards stolen credit card numbers stolen just from being in proximity. And they would be able to read the credit card, just to know those, those money that you just waived. Oh, yeah, exactly. I have them now. My audio just went out. No, no, we can hear you, Michael. Oh, okay. It just, my, my microphone just disappeared and said audio connection out. So yeah. So every bag has this RF technology protection technology now, which is a sign of the times I think that's, that's, well, that's exactly what you're talking about. So. Yeah. I just, just so you know, Gary, Halbert and John Carlton also share the, the bag fetish thing. I have, I have messager bags of my dads that, you know, I collected bags as well. Different kinds of briefcases and day bags and messenger bags and stuff. But I just thought you might like to know that you share that. Oh, and, and, and I remember, I remember seeing him, I remember at one seminar that he came, he wasn't a speaker, he was a guest. And I remember he had bag on one side and then he had his, his Colovian girlfriend on the other side. There you go. I have one more question if you would indulge us from our audience. When you take on a new client, this is, this is technical. Okay. When you take on a new client, do you charge a per hour rate or a percentage based rate scale? Do you have a sliding scale, a per hour scale? The idea is that, you know, what, how should a new copywriter structure? You know, if they're independent and they're freelancing, how should they create that structure and what the other question is, and what do you think is a reasonable rate? Okay. That question is, is, first of all, don't charge either. Charge by the project. Don't charge by the hour. Don't charge a percentage. If you're a brand new copywriter and you don't, you don't have your, you know, you don't have the experience yet, get your, get, you know, you still went behind the ears kind of thing. If you start charging royalties, that's like writing on spec. And that's, that's, that's the worst thing you can ever do because you're going to starve because you're basically paying to learn the process rather than earning a living. So I think what you need to do is charge by the project. And even by the hour, what I tend to do is I go back, I look at how much time I think is going to take me to write a copy, a piece of copy. And I see how much money I would charge on an hourly basis. But then I say, well, here's, and actually I don't even do that. I do buy, buy page now or by number of words, but I don't charge by that. I charge by the project. But to answer your question, what's a reasonable rate? What's a reasonable rate? You know, I remember when I first started out as copy, I did a lot of copy for free or I bartered a lot 25 years ago where I would just write copy for peanuts. I remember my first long copy sales that are 2000, no, 3000 words, 2000, 2600 and I charge 600 bucks for it. You know, and something that I would charge easily 10 grand for now. But my point is don't go with this. Don't go to convert the percentage route just yet. I do, I do three things. One is I only will ask for royalties from a client that I've already written copy for and he's paid me well in the past and I understand their company, I understand their product, I understand their finances. And there's a trust level created because one of the things about royalties is that I need to have access to their finances. I trust no one. I got burnt in the past. I took on clients, brand new clients on a percentage basis on royalty basis. And it sucked. I got, I lost so much money because of that. So that's, that's one thing. The second thing is when you ask for percentage start low. Don't don't don't go ask for much start low and do it as in this is number three as a guaranteed deposit towards royalties. Here's what I mean. Don't ask for just straight up percentage. Say I'm going to try to you X number of dollars for the project plus royalties. And if they if they if there's a if they finagle about that or if they start to to haggle you say, well, didn't do it as a guaranteed deposit. Here's what it means. Ask for the money upfront and say what you're giving me is a guaranteed deposit guarantee means there's no refund. It's a guaranteed deposit applied towards future royalties so that once the royalties accumulate and exceed that deposit, then you pay me those royalties. All right. So and I have, I have a few clients like that. And and how I started doing this was this in the old days where before I asked for 100% upfront, I used to ask for 50 50 50% upfront and then 50% in the back end at the end whenever the sales copy was delivered. So what I would do is when I had a client that I really knew and I love their product and I knew the industry and there was a trust created there. There was a trust level. I would say give me for 50% as a guaranteed deposit, but rather than give me the other 50% give me that in royalties. So that's how that progressed. Now I asked for 100% and I still ask for royalties, but as a guaranteed deposit and the reason why that's great is that they're not paying you. And a lot of clients start to handle with you. They're, they're a few more comfortable because now they know that they're only paying you to make a profit. Right. So, so that is like a win-win situation. That's what I would recommend to this new person. Don't even do that yet. They need to, they need to get their feet wet first. They need to learn how to write copy, how to get paid copy and maybe sometimes even lose. But at the same time, when you get better, then you can start, you know, raising your rates, starting asking for a little bit of royalties. Like start with just simple one. I had one client actually, I remember years ago where I was, my royalty was 1%, but then again, you know, I was writing copy for a cosmetic surgeon whose surgeries were like 10, 20, 30, $40,000. Right. So 1% was nice. Nice chunk of chain. But then going to 5% and 10% and 15 and 25. So start that way and just grow from that point on. But final, final answer is don't charge by the hour. Don't charge by the percentage charged by the project. Good. Fantastic and great practical advice. Thank you so much, Adrian, for asking that question. That's a great one for everybody to think about. We talk about the mechanics of it, but there's also very much a business side and I can guarantee you that everybody whose face you see up here knows all about the business side of it too. So don't be afraid to ask those kinds of questions. They're fantastic. Guys, is there any last thing? It's our last call right now.
